does: This paper studies reinforcement learning from human feedback when human evaluators systematically misspecify rewards on certain contexts. The authors prove that if feedback is biased on a fraction α of contexts with bias strength ε, any learning algorithm requires exponentially many samples exp(n·α·ε²) to distinguish between reward functions that differ only on those problematic inputs. However, they also show that if one has access to a calibration oracle that identifies unreliable feedback regions, the exponential barrier can be overcome with only O(1/(α·ε²)) queries. The main contribution is formally quantifying the hardness of alignment through this exponential sample complexity lower bound and demonstrating that knowing where human feedback fails enables dramatic improvements. This establishes a fundamental tradeoff between the rarity and severity of misspecification versus the sample complexity of learning.
connects: This work extends a line of research on learning with human feedback and specification gaming that includes work on reward misspecification and distribution shift in RL. It builds on prior theoretical analyses of RLHF that have noted the difficulty of learning true objectives from imperfect human judgments, particularly on out-of-distribution or edge-case contexts. The paper connects to broader alignment literature examining why learned proxies diverge from intended objectives, as well as prior work on sample complexity lower bounds in learning theory. It relates to empirical findings that human preferences contain systematic biases and blindspots, formalizing these intuitions within a concrete theoretical framework. The work also connects to research on detecting and correcting errors in learning from demonstrations and on robustness to distributional misspecification.
enables: This work opens several future directions: understanding what types of calibration oracles are realistically achievable and designing methods to learn or approximate them without explicit specification; extending the analysis beyond binary distinctions to continuous or multi-hypothesis settings; studying computational complexity alongside sample complexity; investigating whether active learning or strategic querying of humans near detected failure modes could further reduce query complexity; and exploring practical algorithms that exploit knowledge of problematic contexts. The framework suggests that alignment difficulty fundamentally hinges on making misspecified contexts discoverable, pointing toward research on interpretability methods to identify edge cases, adversarial testing to expose systematic biases in human feedback, and interactive learning procedures that focus human oversight where feedback is most unreliable.
